name: Binary Search
summary: Probe the middle version. If it's bad the answer is at or before it; if it's good the answer is strictly after it. Either way half the versions are eliminated per probe.
visualizationIntent: init: build the list of versions 1..n, all live. move: place left and right at the ends of the search space. loop: while the space has more than one version, glide mid to the midpoint. update: probe mid — the API answer decides which half survives. move: collapse right onto mid (bad) or push left past mid (good); the discarded half dims. return: left and right meet on the first bad version, which flashes green.

lineExplanations.3: Start an empty list to hold the version numbers.
lineExplanations.5: Keep adding versions until we have all n of them.
lineExplanations.6: Add this version number to the list.
lineExplanations.7: Move on to the next version number.
lineExplanations.8: Convert the bad version NUMBER into its list INDEX (versions are 1-based, indexes are 0-based).
lineExplanations.9: The left edge of the live search space — index 0.
lineExplanations.10: The right edge of the live search space — the last index.
lineExplanations.11: Placeholder for the midpoint we're about to probe.
lineExplanations.12: Counts how many API calls we make — the thing we want to minimise.
lineExplanations.13: Holds the API's answer for the current probe.
lineExplanations.15: Keep narrowing while more than one version is still in play.
lineExplanations.16: Pick the midpoint of the live range — written this way to avoid overflow on huge n.
lineExplanations.18: THE API CALL: is version mid bad? Every version from bad_index onward is bad, so this comparison is exactly isBadVersion(mid).
lineExplanations.20: Bad — the first bad version is mid or earlier, so mid becomes the new right edge (we keep mid, it's still a candidate).
lineExplanations.22: Good — the first bad version must be strictly after mid, so left jumps past it.
lineExplanations.23: left and right have met; read the version number at that index.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: is the class wrapper LeetCode expects.
syntaxExplanations.2: def firstBadVersion(self, n, bad): n is how many versions exist; bad is the first broken one (LeetCode hides this inside its isBadVersion API).
syntaxExplanations.4: v = 1 starts the counter at version 1.
syntaxExplanations.5: while v <= n: runs once per version, inclusive of n.
syntaxExplanations.6: append(v) adds v to the end of the list.
syntaxExplanations.7: v = v + 1 moves to the next version number.
syntaxExplanations.8: bad - 1 converts a 1-based version number to a 0-based list index.
syntaxExplanations.9: left = 0 is the first index still in play.
syntaxExplanations.10: n - 1 is the last valid index of a list of length n.
syntaxExplanations.11: mid = 0 declares the variable before the loop uses it.
syntaxExplanations.12: probes = 0 starts the API call counter.
syntaxExplanations.13: is_bad = False declares the boolean before the loop uses it.
syntaxExplanations.15: while left < right: stops as soon as the range holds a single index.
syntaxExplanations.16: left + (right - left) // 2 is the midpoint; // is integer division. Writing it this way instead of (left + right) // 2 avoids overflow in languages with fixed-size ints.
syntaxExplanations.18: mid >= bad_index is True exactly when version mid is bad.
syntaxExplanations.19: if is_bad: branches on the probe result.
syntaxExplanations.20: right = mid keeps mid in the range — it might BE the answer.
syntaxExplanations.21: else: handles the 'version is good' case.
syntaxExplanations.22: left = mid + 1 excludes mid, which is known good.
syntaxExplanations.23: versions[left] reads the version number at the surviving index.
syntaxExplanations.24: return answer hands back the first bad version.
